text:
Most data governance programs are dying because they're scoped wrong.

They get framed as "how do we not get sued" instead of "how do we ship something customers will pay for." Then the program owner reports to legal, the roadmap reads like a risk register, and three years later someone kills it for not generating value.

The way I've started thinking about it: governance is the product, not the guardrail.

Specifically — lineage, access controls, audit trails, and data quality SLAs are paid features in a data product. Not back-office hygiene. The customer (internal or external) is buying trust, and trust has tiers you can price against.

A simple lens I keep coming back to:

If you removed your governance layer tomorrow, would your data product still be sellable? If yes, you've built a feature. If no, you've built infrastructure your buyer doesn't see and won't pay for.

Where this breaks: early-stage data products with no external customer yet. Premature governance there is just bureaucracy in a trench coat. You need real usage patterns before you know which controls are worth charging for.

But once a data product has paying users or regulated downstream consumers, governance stops being a cost center within about two quarters. It becomes the reason renewals happen.

The question I'd actually want answered: in your org, who owns the P&L on governance? If the answer is "nobody," that's probably why it feels like a tax.

#DataMonetization #DataGovernance #DataProducts #ProductStrategy #DaaS #DataStrategy
